docs.title: Kabot Docs
docs.text: # Kabot  A resilient personal AI assistant framework with long-lived memory, native reasoning workflows, multi-channel routing, and an operator-friendly dashboard.   Use this site if you want one path for beginners, one path for operators, and one path for advanced builders without digging through internal planning notes.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
New users Beginner track

Install Kabot, run the setup wizard, start the gateway, and send your first real message.

Operators Dashboard guide

Learn how to use the gateway, dashboard panels, auth scopes, and operator actions safely.

Advanced Advanced track

Dive into runtime architecture, memory design, multi-agent patterns, and integration strategy.

docs.title: What Kabot Is
docs.text:
Kabot is not just a chat wrapper around an LLM. It is a local-first agent framework that combines:

  • persistent conversation and memory systems
  • explicit CLI and gateway operations
  • multi-model and multi-agent routing
  • tool execution with safety layers
  • channels such as Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, and more
  • operator surfaces for live monitoring and management

docs.title: What You Can Do With It
docs.text:
Personal assistant

Run Kabot on your PC, Mac mini, VPS, or Termux device and chat through CLI or messaging channels.

Operator dashboard

Monitor sessions, channels, cron jobs, model usage, system health, and cost windows from one web UI.

AI workflow engine

Use Kabot for planning, coding, debugging, research, automation, reminders, and background tasks.

Multi-agent runtime

Split work across separate agents with different workspaces, models, and channel bindings.

docs.title: Recommended Reading Order
docs.text:
1. Beginner track
  2. Install
  3. First run
  4. Quickstart
  5. Configuration guide
  6. Gateway and dashboard guide
  7. Advanced overview

docs.title: Docs Scope
docs.text:
This MkDocs site is the curated public docs surface.

It intentionally focuses on: - onboarding - usage - operator workflows - advanced architecture - practical reference

It intentionally does not dump the internal docs/plans/ archive into the main navigation.

docs.title: Advanced Docs
docs.text: This section is for operators, power users, and contributors who want to understand how Kabot behaves under the hood.

docs.title: What You Will Find Here
docs.text:
• runtime architecture
  • operator workflow patterns
  • integrations and extension strategy
  • mental models for performance, safety, and maintainability

docs.title: Who Should Read This
docs.text: Use this section if you are: - running Kabot continuously - managing multiple channels or agents - debugging runtime behavior - comparing Kabot with other agent frameworks - extending Kabot rather than just using it

docs.title: Suggested Order
docs.text:
1. Runtime Architecture
  2. Operator Patterns
  3. Integrations and Extensions

docs.title: Integrations and Extensions
docs.text: Kabot has two broad extension paths.

docs.title: Path 1: Native Product Surfaces
docs.text:
These are built-in product areas such as: - gateway - dashboard - channels - native Google flows - built-in tools and runtime handlers

Choose this path when you need something that deeply affects: - routing - operator UI - webhook handling - background runtime behavior

docs.title: Path 2: Skills and External Workflows
docs.text: Choose skills or external capabilities when you need: - a guided workflow - a specific API integration - a task-specific capability that does not require a new core runtime surface

docs.title: When A Core Code Change Is Actually Needed
docs.text: A core change is more likely necessary when you need: - a new webhook listener - a new long-running service or daemon behavior - a brand-new dashboard/operator surface - deep runtime or routing changes

docs.title: Good Skill Candidate
docs.text:
• new productivity integration with API key auth
  • repo helper that uses existing filesystem and shell tools
  • workflow assistant for a narrow domain

docs.title: Good Core Candidate
docs.text:
• new first-class inbound channel
  • new dashboard monitoring family
  • new runtime policy layer

docs.title: Documentation Advice For Extensions
docs.text: If you add a major extension path, document all of these: - setup - auth - runtime assumptions - failure modes - how to verify it works

docs.title: Operator Patterns
docs.text: This page describes practical ways to run Kabot well over time.

docs.title: Pattern 1: One Stable Test Prompt Set
docs.text:
Keep a small set of known-good prompts for: - temporal response - filesystem navigation - model override - dashboard chat - one inbound channel message

Run them after meaningful config or runtime changes.

docs.title: Pattern 2: Separate Observation From Mutation
docs.text:
In the dashboard and gateway world, first observe, then mutate.

Good sequence: 1. open dashboard 2. inspect health, sessions, and nodes 3. confirm scope/auth mode 4. then use write actions

docs.title: Pattern 3: One Change At A Time
docs.text:
Do not change: - model chain - channel binding - gateway auth token - memory mode

all at once.

Change one thing, smoke test, then continue.

docs.title: Pattern 4: Scope-Aware Tokens
docs.text: Use: - operator.read for observation-only operators - operator.write only where mutation is necessary - ingress.write only where webhook ingestion is needed

docs.title: Pattern 5: Role-Based Agents
docs.text:
Use multiple agents when roles genuinely differ.

Examples: - support agent for customer-facing channels - research agent for long-form exploratory tasks - work agent for coding and operations

docs.title: Pattern 6: Treat Skills As Capabilities, Not Magic
docs.text: A skill still needs: - relevant env keys - any required binaries - a good runtime path - realistic operator expectations

docs.title: Pattern 7: Use Doctor And Smoke Checks Proactively
docs.text:
Operator confidence comes from repeatable checks, not intuition.

kabot doctor --fix
kabot doctor smoke-agent --smoke-timeout 30

docs.title: Runtime Architecture
docs.text: Kabot is built as a layered runtime rather than a single chat loop.

docs.title: CLI and Gateway Surfaces
docs.text: The outer layer exposes Kabot through: - CLI commands - gateway routes - dashboard/operator surfaces - channel adapters

docs.title: Agent Runtime
docs.text: The agent runtime is responsible for: - prompt and context assembly - skill matching and workflow steering - routing and semantic intent handling - tool selection or suppression - model fallback behavior

docs.title: Tool and Execution Runtime
docs.text: This layer handles: - direct-tool fast paths - simple no-tool responses - guarded execution loops - tool result handling - provider/model retry logic

docs.title: Memory Layer
docs.text: The memory system can use lightweight or hybrid strategies and now includes lazy probe behavior to keep cold-start costs down for one-shot runs.

docs.title: AI-Driven, Not Template-Driven
docs.text:
Kabot tries to keep the assistant natural instead of turning every prompt into a hardcoded command parser.

Where deterministic behavior is necessary, it is generally used narrowly for: - direct file/path actions - tiny temporal replies - safety or operator-critical routing

docs.title: Direct Paths For High-Confidence Cases
docs.text:
Recent runtime work introduced faster direct paths for: - tiny temporal prompts - deterministic filesystem listings - safe direct-tool actions

This helps latency without forcing the whole product into a rigid rule engine.

docs.title: Refactor-For-Stability Approach
docs.text: Major runtime files were split into smaller package parts so behavior stayed intact while maintainability improved. That matters because Kabot is now large enough that runtime clarity directly affects correctness.

docs.title: Why This Matters Operationally
docs.text: When Kabot feels fast, natural, and stable, it is usually because these layers are doing the right thing together: - context stays small enough - routing avoids unnecessary LLM hops - deterministic paths are used only where confidence is high - tool and model retries stay observable and bounded

docs.title: Beginner Track
docs.text: This section is for people who want the shortest reliable path from zero to a working Kabot instance.

docs.title: The 5-Step Path
docs.text:
1. Install Kabot.
  2. Run kabot config.
  3. Choose at least one model provider.
  4. Start kabot gateway.
  5. Test with kabot agent -m "Hello Kabot".

docs.title: Who This Track Is For
docs.text: Use this track if you are: - new to local AI tooling - not sure which config options matter yet - trying Kabot on Windows, macOS, Linux, or Termux - more interested in getting it running than reading architecture first

docs.title: Beginner Success Criteria
docs.text: You are done with the beginner track when: - the setup wizard saves without errors - kabot gateway starts cleanly - the dashboard opens - a one-shot CLI message works - you understand where to edit config later

docs.title: Reading Order
docs.text:
1. Install
  2. First Run
  3. Quickstart
  4. Dashboard

docs.title: If You Want The Shortest Version
docs.text:
Install 
pip install -U kabot
kabot config
kabot gateway
kabot agent -m "Hello Kabot"

docs.title: Dashboard For Beginners
docs.text: The Kabot dashboard is the operator web surface for monitoring, chat, and quick actions.

docs.title: What You Will See
docs.text: Common sections include: - overview and runtime health - chat panel - sessions - nodes - config snapshot - control actions - cost and usage panels - charts and model breakdowns - channels, cron jobs, skills, and models

docs.title: Opening The Dashboard
docs.text:
http://127.0.0.1:<port>/dashboard

If auth is enabled, use the dashboard token flow documented in the gateway guide.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Use this to confirm: - gateway is alive - uptime is moving - sessions and nodes exist - alerts are clear

docs.title: Chat
docs.text: Use this when you want: - a browser-based operator chat panel - model override tests - prompt/send flow without leaving the dashboard

docs.title: Settings
docs.text: Use this to: - inspect config - review control actions - understand which routes are read-only versus write-capable

docs.title: Good Beginner Habits
docs.text:
• Start with read-only observation first.
  • Use write actions only after you understand your auth scope.
  • Keep one stable test session for verifying behavior after config changes.

docs.title: Next Reading
docs.text: Move to the full Gateway and Dashboard guide when you are ready for auth scopes, operator actions, and panel details.

docs.title: Step 1: Open The Setup Wizard
docs.text:
kabot config

This is the main setup surface for most users.

docs.title: Step 2: Configure A Model Provider
docs.text:
Inside the wizard, start with Model / Auth.

Recommended beginner flow: - login to one provider first - let Kabot auto-build a primary model path - only add manual override models after the basics work

Good first providers for many users: - OpenAI - Anthropic - Gemini - Groq - OpenRouter - Ollama (Local Auto-Discovery)

docs.title: Step 3: Save Configuration
docs.text: Once the wizard saves: - Kabot writes config into your local runtime area - provider credentials are stored locally - later menus like Channels, Skills, and Gateway can reuse that config

docs.title: Step 4: Start The Gateway
docs.text:
kabot gateway

Expected result: - Kabot starts listening on the configured gateway port - dashboard routes become available - channel/webhook ingress becomes available if configured

docs.title: Step 5: Test CLI Chat
docs.text:
kabot agent -m "Hello Kabot"

This verifies: - CLI entrypoint works - your model config is usable - Kabot can build runtime context and answer

docs.title: If Something Fails
docs.text:
Go to: - Configuration guide - Troubleshooting

And run:

kabot doctor --fix

docs.title: Important Mental Model
docs.text:
Kabot has a few core surfaces:

  • kabot config for setup and operator configuration
  • kabot gateway for the web/dashboard and ingress runtime
  • kabot agent for direct chat and one-shot prompts
  • kabot doctor for diagnostics and smoke checks

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.11 or newer
  • Internet access for model APIs and package install
  • A supported OS:
  • Windows
  • macOS
  • Linux
  • Termux on Android

docs.title: Standard Install
docs.text:
pip install -U kabot

If pip is not on your path:

python -m pip install -U kabot

docs.title: Recommended
docs.text: py -m pip install -U kabot

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use PowerShell, Windows Terminal, or a UTF-8 capable shell.
  • If you use virtual environments, activate them before running Kabot.
  • For direct one-shot commands with non-ASCII prompts, modern UTF-8 shells work best.

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
python3 -m pip install -U kabot

Tips: - Use a virtual environment if you do local development. - If Homebrew Python is installed, prefer that Python explicitly.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
python3 -m pip install -U kabot

Tips: - On Debian/Ubuntu, make sure python3-venv is available if you want a virtual environment. - For long-running installs, use a dedicated user rather than root.

docs.title: Termux
docs.text:
pkg update && pkg upgrade
pkg install python git clang make libjpeg-turbo freetype rust
pip install -U pip
pip install -U kabot

Read the full Termux guidance before using hybrid memory on low-RAM devices.

docs.title: Developer Install
docs.text:
Use this only if you want to change source code locally.

git clone https://github.com/kaivyy/kabot.git
cd kabot
python -m venv .venv

Activate:

# Windows
.venv\Scripts\activate

# macOS / Linux
source .venv/bin/activate

Then install editable mode:

pip install -e .

docs.title: Optional Docs Tooling
docs.text:
If you want to build this docs site locally:

pip install ".[docs]"
mkdocs serve

docs.title: Verify Install
docs.text:
kabot --help
python -m kabot.cli.commands --version

If both commands work, continue to First Run.

docs.title: Fastest Reliable Path
docs.text:
pip install -U kabot
kabot config
kabot gateway
kabot agent -m "Hello Kabot"

docs.title: 1. Start the gateway
docs.text: kabot gateway

docs.title: 2. Open the dashboard
docs.text:
Open:

http://127.0.0.1:<port>/dashboard

If gateway auth token is enabled, use the tokenized URL or bearer auth as configured.

docs.title: 3. Send a test prompt
docs.text: kabot agent -m "What can you help me with?"

docs.title: 4. Try a practical prompt
docs.text: kabot agent -m "Summarize my current Kabot setup goals in 5 bullets."

docs.title: First Things To Configure After Basic Success
docs.text:
1. Add a fallback model.
  2. Open the Gateway menu and set a stable port and auth token.
  3. Decide whether you want channels like Telegram or WhatsApp.
  4. Decide whether you want hybrid memory enabled.
  5. Run a health check.
kabot doctor --fix
kabot doctor smoke-agent --smoke-timeout 30

docs.title: Milestone 1
docs.text:
• setup wizard saved
  • one-shot agent works

docs.title: Milestone 2
docs.text:
• dashboard opens
  • token/auth access is clear

docs.title: Milestone 3
docs.text:
• one channel connected
  • one successful end-to-end message

docs.title: Milestone 4
docs.text: • you understand where config, memory, and dashboard fit together

docs.title: Channels Guide
docs.text: Channels let people talk to Kabot from real messaging surfaces instead of only the CLI.

docs.title: What Channels Are For
docs.text:
Channels are how Kabot becomes operational in the real world.

Instead of asking every user to open a local shell, you can expose Kabot through: - direct messaging bots - team chat platforms - bridge-backed chat services - channel-specific agents

Good use cases: - a personal Telegram bot - a Discord helper for your server - an internal Slack assistant - a WhatsApp-based support or family workflow

docs.title: Common Channel Types
docs.text: Kabot supports a broad set of channels and adapters, including: - Telegram - Discord - Slack - WhatsApp bridge flows - Feishu, DingTalk, QQ, email - additional bridge-style and promoted adapters depending on configuration

docs.title: Beginner Mental Model
docs.text:
Each channel setup usually answers four questions:

  1. How does Kabot authenticate to the platform?
  2. Who is allowed to talk to the bot?
  3. Which agent should receive those messages?
  4. Does the platform need a local bridge or direct API access?

If you can answer those four clearly, channel setup becomes much easier.

docs.title: What Channels Need
docs.text:
Most channels need three things: - credentials or bot token - access control rules such as allowFrom - optional binding to a specific agent

In practice, some channels also need: - webhook or bridge health - local background service availability - per-channel message policy - per-instance model or agent routing

docs.title: Recommended Setup Order
docs.text:
For a stable rollout, do this in order:

  1. make sure kabot agent -m "hello" works first
  2. start kabot gateway
  3. connect one channel only
  4. test one inbound message
  5. test one outbound response
  6. add allowFrom
  7. only then add more channels or bots

docs.title: Use The Wizard First
docs.text:
kabot config

Then go into Channels.

This is the safest path for: - adding one channel - managing multiple instances - setting allowFrom - choosing per-bot AI routing

The wizard matters because it reduces: - token leaks in prompts - malformed manual JSON edits - forgotten allow lists - broken per-bot bindings

docs.title: Single-Bot
docs.text: Best when: - you are just starting - one persona is enough - one model is enough - the channel is personal use only

docs.title: Multi-Bot / Multi-Instance
docs.text: Best when: - different groups need different behavior - one bot should be cheap and fast, another more capable - you want strong separation between work and personal contexts - you want different agents per channel or per chat surface

docs.title: Multi-Bot Setup
docs.text:
You can run multiple channel instances and bind them differently.

Examples: - Telegram bot A -> work agent - Telegram bot B -> personal agent - WhatsApp bridge -> support agent

This is especially useful when each bot should have: - a different model - a different workspace - a different role

docs.title: Channel Security Basics
docs.text:
The most important security field for most channel setups is allowFrom.

That field controls who may talk to the bot.

Examples: - Telegram user IDs - WhatsApp phone numbers - allow-list style identities depending on the adapter

Good rule: - personal toy bot: still use allow lists if possible - shared or public deployment: treat allow lists as mandatory

docs.title: Telegram
docs.text:
Good first channel for most users because: - setup is straightforward - debugging is simple - one-to-one testing is easy

Recommended use: - direct personal bot - operator testing - first real-world smoke tests

docs.title: Discord
docs.text:
Good when: - you want Kabot inside a community or team space - mentions or channel workflows matter

Things to think about: - permission scope - channel visibility - bot role placement

docs.title: Slack
docs.text:
Good for internal team workflows.

Pay attention to: - bot token vs app token requirements - DM policy - group policy - workspace restrictions

docs.title: WhatsApp
docs.text:
Usually the most operationally sensitive because it often depends on a local bridge.

Things to verify: - bridge is reachable - QR login succeeded - bridge can stay alive in the background - local machine can maintain that runtime

docs.title: Email / Bridge-Based Adapters
docs.text: These often need a more careful operator mindset because they may involve: - separate bridge health - external auth systems - more failure points than a single direct bot token

docs.title: Security Rules
docs.text:
For production or shared deployments: - configure allowFrom - audit who can reach each bot - do not leave public channels open without a clear policy

Also consider: - using dedicated agents per sensitive channel - avoiding a single bot identity for everything - using least-privilege gateway and operator tokens

docs.title: Agent Binding Strategy
docs.text:
Channel bindings become much easier to manage if you decide the role first.

Examples:

Channel Suggested Agent Role Telegram personal bot default personal agent Discord community helper moderation or community agent Slack internal workspace work or ops agent WhatsApp support bot support or triage agent

docs.title: Testing Checklist
docs.text:
After configuring a channel, verify all of these:

  1. credential saved
  2. channel appears configured
  3. one allowed sender can reach it
  4. one blocked sender cannot reach it
  5. the correct agent receives the message
  6. the reply reaches the right place

docs.title: The bot is silent
docs.text: Check: - credential validity - gateway is running - channel bridge is healthy - allowFrom is not blocking you - the binding points to the expected agent

docs.title: The wrong agent replies
docs.text: Check: - channel binding order - default agent fallback - instance-specific routing

docs.title: Messages are inconsistent
docs.text: Check: - whether multiple bots share one role accidentally - whether one channel is bound to the wrong workspace - whether bridge-based adapters are unstable

docs.title: Recommended Channel Rollout
docs.text:
1. Start with one channel.
  2. Verify one real inbound and outbound message.
  3. Add allowFrom restrictions.
  4. Only then add extra bots or extra platforms.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Configuration guide
  • Multi-agent guide
  • Gateway and dashboard guide
  • Security guide

docs.title: CLI Guide
docs.text: The CLI is the fastest way to test Kabot and the most direct way to operate it from a terminal.

docs.title: Most Important Commands
docs.text:
kabot config
kabot gateway
kabot agent -m "Hello Kabot"
kabot doctor --fix

docs.title: One-Shot Prompts
docs.text:
kabot agent -m "Summarize today's priorities"

Use one-shot mode when you want: - fast testing - scripting - automation hooks - smoke checks

docs.title: Interactive Mode
docs.text:
kabot agent

Use interactive mode when you want: - a longer conversation - follow-up context inside the same session - repeated manual testing

docs.title: Health And Diagnostics
docs.text:
kabot doctor --fix
kabot doctor routing
kabot doctor smoke-agent --smoke-timeout 30

These help when: - startup feels wrong - routing seems inconsistent - you want a quick agent verification loop

docs.title: Auth And Model Management
docs.text:
Common patterns include:

kabot auth status
kabot auth login openai
kabot auth login google --method oauth

docs.title: Agent And Multi-Agent Management
docs.text:
kabot agents list
kabot agents add work --name "Work Agent" --model openai/gpt-4o

docs.title: Good CLI Habits
docs.text:
• use one-shot prompts for reproducible checks
  • use interactive mode for exploratory conversations
  • keep a note of your known-good test prompt set
  • re-run doctor smoke-agent after risky runtime changes

docs.title: Advanced CLI Note
docs.text: Recent Kabot improvements make one-shot runs safer by isolating ad-hoc prompts from stale default session context when no explicit session is provided.

docs.title: Configuration Guide
docs.text: This page explains how to think about Kabot configuration, not just where to click.

docs.title: Main Entry Point
docs.text:
kabot config

That wizard is the canonical setup path for most users.

docs.title: Model / Auth
docs.text:
Controls: - provider login - model selection - fallback chains - provider-specific auth methods

Key idea: - get one provider working first - add fallback after success - only then fine-tune overrides

docs.title: Gateway
docs.text: Controls: - bind host and port - auth token and scopes - exposure mode - dashboard availability - Tailscale-related deployment behavior

docs.title: Skills
docs.text: Controls: - skill enable and disable state - skill env variables - install/setup planning for third-party dependencies

docs.title: Channels
docs.text: Controls: - bot/channel credentials - allowFrom access rules - multi-bot and instance workflows - channel-to-agent bindings

docs.title: Memory
docs.text: Controls: - memory backend choices - persistence behavior - hybrid or lightweight paths depending on machine limits

docs.title: Good Beginner Defaults
docs.text:
• one primary model
  • one fallback model
  • gateway auth token enabled
  • strict or balanced execution policy
  • start with one channel only
  • enable more advanced surfaces only after a stable first run

docs.title: Where Config Matters Most
docs.text: Area Why it matters Model/Auth determines whether Kabot can answer at all Gateway controls operator access and web runtime Channels controls who can talk to Kabot Memory changes performance and recall behavior Skills changes which advanced capabilities can activate

docs.title: Safe Change Workflow
docs.text:
1. Make one config change.
  2. Save.
  3. Run a quick smoke check.
  4. Test one real prompt.
  5. Only then move to the next change.
kabot doctor --fix
kabot agent -m "say hello in one line"

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Authentication reference
  • CLI guide
  • Gateway and dashboard guide

docs.title: Gateway and Dashboard Guide
docs.text: The gateway is Kabot's HTTP surface for dashboard access, webhook ingress, and operator actions.

docs.title: Start The Gateway
docs.text:
kabot gateway

By default, Kabot uses the configured gateway port unless you override it for a single run.

docs.title: Dashboard Access
docs.text:
Without auth token:

http://127.0.0.1:<port>/dashboard

With auth token enabled: - use the dashboard tokenized URL pattern - or use bearer auth for API clients

docs.title: What The Dashboard Is For
docs.text: The dashboard is the operator surface for: - runtime visibility - quick chat - sessions and nodes management - config inspection - cron and skills controls - usage and model monitoring

docs.title: Auth Scope Model
docs.text:
Kabot supports scoped gateway auth tokens.

Important scope families: - operator.read - operator.write - ingress.write

Practical meaning: - operator.read lets you see dashboard/status surfaces - operator.write lets you perform dashboard write actions - ingress.write controls webhook ingress routes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Health, metrics, alerts, uptime, sessions, nodes, cost windows.

docs.title: Chat
docs.text: Operator chat with model routing and fallback control.

docs.title: Sessions and Nodes
docs.text: Runtime visibility and action buttons where permitted by scope.

docs.title: Settings / Config / Control
docs.text: Useful for checking token mode, control surfaces, and read-only versus write-capable behavior.

docs.title: Monitoring Panels
docs.text: Recent parity work added panels for: - health - cost and usage - charts - channels - cron jobs - models - skills - sub-agent activity - git log snapshots

docs.title: Performance Notes
docs.text:
The dashboard is designed to avoid unnecessary full reloads.

Recent improvements include: - cached status snapshots for bursts of panel requests - active-tab preservation during auto-refresh - sticky chat scroll behavior - SSE-backed chat updates - centralized refresh behavior instead of every panel polling independently

docs.title: Security Recommendations
docs.text:
• enable gateway auth token for any non-trivial deployment
  • do not expose write-capable routes unnecessarily
  • use Tailscale or a reverse proxy rather than a broad open bind when possible
  • prefer least-privilege scoped tokens

docs.title: Related References
docs.text:
• Dashboard routes reference
  • Security guide
  • Configuration guide

docs.title: Google Suite Guide
docs.text: Kabot supports a native Google integration path.

docs.title: Important Clarification
docs.text:
Kabot's Google Suite setup path is native.

It does not require: - npm - Node.js - gog

That makes it the recommended path for most users who simply want Google auth and Google actions from Kabot.

docs.title: Typical Flow
docs.text:
1. Run kabot config.
  2. Open Google Suite.
  3. Provide the required Google credentials path.
  4. Complete the consent flow.
  5. Save and verify access.

docs.title: What It Can Support
docs.text: Depending on your configured features and flows, Google access can be used for: - email-related actions - calendar workflows - Drive/Docs style operations - broader Google-backed automation tasks

docs.title: Native vs Skill-Based Google Flows
docs.text:
Use native Google Suite when: - you want the simplest supported auth path - you do not want extra Node ecosystem dependencies - you want the built-in Kabot path

Use a skill-based flow only when: - you need a specialized external tool or workflow that goes beyond the native integration - you understand that it may have its own dependency and auth model

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: For beginners, always start with native Google Suite first.

docs.title: Memory Guide
docs.text: Kabot supports multiple memory strategies so you can choose between capability and footprint.

docs.title: Main Memory Idea
docs.text:
Kabot is designed to avoid the usual "stateless chatbot" feel.

Depending on configuration, Kabot can retain: - session history - useful facts and preferences - retrieved contextual knowledge - graph-like relationship context

The goal is not to hoard every message forever.

The goal is to keep the right information available at the right time without destroying latency or token budget.

docs.title: Lightweight / Simple Paths
docs.text: Best when you need: - low RAM usage - simpler environments - reduced overhead on small machines

docs.title: Hybrid Memory
docs.text: Best when you need: - stronger retrieval quality - richer context reuse - longer-term knowledge behavior - better semantic and keyword blending

docs.title: How To Choose
docs.text:
Use this rough rule:

Situation Better Starting Choice laptop with limited RAM lightweight path small VPS lightweight path Termux lightweight path first personal workstation with more headroom hybrid memory long-running knowledge-heavy workflow hybrid memory

docs.title: Why Memory Choice Matters
docs.text:
Memory affects: - latency - RAM/disk footprint - retrieval quality - how much context Kabot can reuse across runs

It also affects how Kabot feels: - quick and light - or deeper and more context-rich

docs.title: Operational Advice
docs.text:
Use lightweight memory first if you are on: - low-RAM laptops - VPS with strict resource limits - Termux devices

Use hybrid memory when you want: - stronger recall - better semantic retrieval - more advanced project continuity

docs.title: What Memory Is Not
docs.text:
Memory is not a guarantee that Kabot will perfectly remember every conversational correction forever.

Real behavior depends on: - session continuity - what memory mode is active - retrieval thresholds - runtime path - whether the relevant fact was persisted or only present in short-lived context

docs.title: Architecture Direction
docs.text:
Kabot's memory architecture uses layered ideas such as: - persistent stores for history/facts - hybrid retrieval strategies - reranking and token-guard behavior - lazy initialization paths to reduce cold-start cost

This is why some recent runtime work focused on: - lazy probe memory paths - lighter one-shot startup - better balance between memory power and cold-start speed

docs.title: Recent Runtime Improvements
docs.text:
Recent work improved memory-related startup behavior by introducing lighter probe paths for one-shot runs before heavy memory systems are needed.

That means simple one-shot prompts do not always have to pay the full cost of booting a heavier memory stack immediately.

docs.title: Memory And Performance
docs.text:
If Kabot feels slow, memory may be only one part of the reason.

Latency can come from: - provider/model response time - context assembly - skill loading - memory initialization - retrieval and reranking cost

Good debugging sequence:

  1. verify model latency separately
  2. test one-shot prompt
  3. compare with interactive session
  4. test lighter memory mode
  5. only then decide whether memory is the main bottleneck

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Usually fine for normal local usage, but heavy embedding or indexing workloads still depend on machine size.

docs.title: macOS / Linux
docs.text: Often good for always-on background runs, especially on a mini server or workstation.

docs.title: Termux
docs.text: Needs the most care: - lighter models - lighter memory - smaller expectations for always-on heavy retrieval

docs.title: Good Practices
docs.text:
• start light
  • measure first
  • only enable heavier memory paths when they solve a real problem
  • keep one or two smoke prompts for memory-sensitive checks

docs.title: When To Go Advanced
docs.text: Move to the advanced/runtime docs when you want to reason about: - hybrid retrieval trade-offs - memory architecture design - startup optimization - long-running project continuity strategies

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Troubleshooting
  • Advanced runtime architecture
  • Multi-agent guide

docs.title: If You See Memory-Related Slowdowns
docs.text:
• reduce advanced memory load
  • test one-shot prompts separately
  • use kabot doctor smoke-agent
  • prefer lighter models and smaller memory footprints on constrained machines

docs.title: Multi-Agent Guide
docs.text: Kabot can run multiple agents with separate workspaces, models, and routing rules.

docs.title: Why Use Multiple Agents
docs.text:
Use multiple agents when one bot should not do everything.

Examples: - work agent - personal agent - support agent - research agent - automation agent

docs.title: What Stays Separate Per Agent
docs.text:
• workspace
  • session history
  • model path
  • bindings and usage context

docs.title: Simple Example
docs.text:
agents:
  list:
    - id: work
      name: Work Agent
      model: openai/gpt-4o
      workspace: ~/.kabot/workspace-work
    - id: personal
      name: Personal Agent
      model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5
      workspace: ~/.kabot/workspace-personal
      default: true

docs.title: Routing Basics
docs.text: Messages generally resolve by: 1. exact binding 2. channel binding 3. default agent fallback

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• give each agent a clear role
  • keep one default agent
  • use separate workspaces
  • avoid overloading one agent with every channel and every task

docs.title: When Multi-Agent Is Worth It
docs.text: Choose multi-agent when: - different channels need different models - different users need different personas or boundaries - you want cleaner memory separation - you need dedicated role-based automation

docs.title: Security Guide
docs.text: Kabot can execute tools, hold credentials, and expose gateway surfaces, so security choices matter.

docs.title: First Principles
docs.text:
• never commit secrets
  • prefer least privilege
  • treat allowFrom and auth tokens as mandatory in real deployments
  • avoid running Kabot as a highly privileged system user

docs.title: API Keys And Tokens
docs.text: Recommendations: - keep credentials local - rotate sensitive keys regularly - use dedicated keys per environment where possible - do not paste secrets into source-controlled files

docs.title: Gateway Security
docs.text: For any non-trivial deployment: - enable gateway auth token - use scoped tokens instead of broad all-access tokens - expose the dashboard carefully - prefer safer network surfaces such as Tailscale or a controlled reverse proxy

docs.title: Channel Security
docs.text:
Always think about who can send messages to your bot.

Use: - allowFrom - DM/group policy controls where applicable - explicit review of who is permitted

docs.title: Tool Execution Security
docs.text:
Kabot can execute commands and work with files. That is powerful and requires operational judgment.

Recommended habits: - review what tools are enabled - use safer execution policies where possible - avoid unnecessary write/destructive permissions - run Kabot under a user account appropriate for its job

docs.title: Production Checklist
docs.text:
• auth token enabled
  • scoped gateway access configured
  • allowFrom configured
  • logs monitored
  • updates applied
  • provider usage limits set
  • backup plan for config and workspace

docs.title: Security Reporting
docs.text: If you discover a vulnerability, report it privately rather than opening a public exploit issue.

docs.title: Skills Guide
docs.text: Skills are one of the main ways Kabot becomes more capable without turning into a rigid bot.

docs.title: What A Skill Is
docs.text:
A skill is a structured capability package that can teach Kabot how to approach a task or workflow.

Skills can range from: - planning and debugging workflows - messaging and channel tasks - media and integrations - coding and operational tasks

docs.title: The Right Mental Model
docs.text:
A skill is not the same as: - a plugin - a model - a bot persona - a shell script

A skill is better thought of as a capability contract: - when it should be used - how the agent should behave - what setup is required - what external tools or APIs it expects

docs.title: Types Of Skills
docs.text: In practice, skills usually fall into one of three buckets:

docs.title: 1. Instruction-Only Skills
docs.text:
These mostly steer the agent's behavior and tool usage.

Examples: - planning workflows - debugging workflows - code review workflows

docs.title: 2. Integration Skills
docs.text: These connect Kabot to real external services, often with: - env keys - OAuth - binaries - API docs

docs.title: 3. Workspace Skills
docs.text: These are custom capabilities created specifically for your own workspace, team, or domain.

docs.title: What The Skills Menu Does
docs.text: In kabot config, the Skills section is for: - enabling or disabling skills - filling in required environment values - understanding which skills need external setup - planning dependency installs

docs.title: What The Skills Menu Does Not Do
docs.text:
It does not blindly run package-manager installs for everything.

That is intentional.

Kabot separates: - skill definition discovery - skill configuration - dependency planning - actual tool/runtime installation

That separation is healthy because it prevents: - accidental installs you did not mean to run - confusing setup states - broken assumptions about external runtimes already being available

docs.title: Skill Labels You May See
docs.text:
• needs env
  • needs binary
  • needs oauth
  • needs node package

These are setup hints, not failures.

docs.title: What Those Labels Mean In Practice
docs.text: Label Meaning needs env the skill expects env values or API keys needs binary an external executable must exist on the machine needs oauth you must complete an auth/login flow needs node package dependency planning likely touches Node ecosystem tooling

docs.title: Beginner Workflow For Skills
docs.text:
1. start with built-in/native flows first
  2. enable only the skills you understand
  3. fill required env values
  4. read the skill's setup assumptions
  5. test one narrow use case

Good beginner question: - \"Do I need this skill right now, or is native Kabot functionality already enough?\"

docs.title: Auto-Selected Skills
docs.text:
Kabot can match relevant skills to a prompt automatically.

Recent runtime improvements keep this more efficient by: - using faster matching paths for explicit skill requests - avoiding full skill-catalog bloat on lightweight prompts - preserving AI-driven responses instead of forcing stiff hardcoded behavior

This matters because the goal is: - natural conversation first - deterministic help only where confidence is high - less prompt bloat - better runtime speed

docs.title: Explicit Skill Requests
docs.text:
You can also ask Kabot to use a skill more directly.

Examples: - please use the weather skill for this request - tolong pakai skill weather untuk ini - use the debugging workflow before changing code

Recent runtime work made these explicit skill turns much lighter, so they no longer need to drag the full skill catalog into the prompt.

docs.title: Creating New Skills
docs.text:
Kabot can also help create or install skills through guided conversation.

Good example prompts: - create a new skill for Threads posting - install a skill from this GitHub repo - show me installable curated skills

Expected flow: 1. discovery questions 2. short plan 3. your approval 4. execution

That approval step matters.

Kabot should not jump straight from: - vague idea to - file creation and mutation

without a plan and your go-ahead.

docs.title: Installing Third-Party Skills
docs.text:
There is a difference between: - built-in skills already shipped with Kabot - workspace skills you create - external skills pulled from another repo

For external skills, always think about: - trust - env requirements - binaries needed - who will maintain the dependency later

docs.title: Mistake 1: Assuming skill enabled means fully ready
docs.text: A skill may be enabled but still not operational because: - env keys are missing - external CLI is missing - OAuth is incomplete

docs.title: Mistake 2: Treating skills as magic parsers
docs.text: Skills improve behavior and capability, but they do not remove the need for: - clear prompts - proper setup - correct runtime environment

docs.title: Mistake 3: Installing too much too early
docs.text: Start with a small set of useful skills, not everything.

docs.title: Good Skill Strategy
docs.text: For most users:

docs.title: Phase 1
docs.text:
• use native Kabot first
  • add only one or two key skills

docs.title: Phase 2
docs.text: • add integration skills that clearly solve a real need

docs.title: Phase 3
docs.text: • create workspace-specific skills for your own workflows

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Google Suite guide
  • Configuration guide
  • Advanced integrations

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• enable only what you understand
  • fill in env keys before expecting a skill to work fully
  • treat third-party installs as real operational dependencies
  • keep native built-ins and external skill tooling conceptually separate

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: This page is for the most common failures when getting Kabot running or stable.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
kabot doctor --fix
kabot doctor routing
kabot doctor smoke-agent --smoke-timeout 30

docs.title: Problem: CLI Works Poorly Or Feels Inconsistent
docs.text:
Check: - model auth is valid - one-shot prompts are using the expected config - your shell is not damaging Unicode input

Use one short test:

kabot agent -m "hari apa sekarang? jawab singkat"

docs.title: Problem: Gateway Starts But Dashboard Feels Wrong
docs.text: Check: - correct port - correct auth token or query token - active dashboard scope (operator.read vs operator.write) - any reverse proxy or bind mode mismatch

docs.title: Problem: Channels Are Silent
docs.text: Check: - token/credential validity - allowFrom - channel binding to the right agent - bridge health for bridge-based adapters

docs.title: Problem: Memory Is Heavy Or Slow
docs.text: Try: - a lighter memory path - lighter model choices - one-shot smoke checks before interactive deep sessions - Termux-specific low-RAM adjustments

docs.title: Problem: Non-ASCII Prompts In Windows Shells
docs.text: Kabot now has runtime hardening for mojibake-style shell input damage, but modern UTF-8 capable terminals are still the safest path.

docs.title: Termux And Low-RAM Notes
docs.text: On constrained devices: - prefer lighter models - avoid unnecessary heavy memory features at first - test with one channel and one model before expanding

docs.title: When To Escalate
docs.text: Collect: - the exact command - the exact error - whether it happens only in CLI, only in dashboard, or both - whether it is tied to one model/provider or all of them

docs.title: Authentication Reference
docs.text: Kabot supports multiple authentication styles depending on provider.

docs.title: Common Modes
docs.text:
• API key
  • OAuth
  • device-style flow for some providers
  • provider-specific methods such as setup tokens or subscription codes

docs.title: Practical Commands
docs.text:
kabot auth status
kabot auth login openai
kabot auth login google --method oauth

docs.title: Important Advice
docs.text:
• use one provider first
  • verify it works before adding more
  • prefer the native supported auth path before adding skill-based alternatives
  • treat expired tokens as runtime issues to diagnose, not mysterious AI failures

docs.title: Related Guide
docs.text: See the main Configuration guide and Google Suite guide.

docs.title: Commands Reference
docs.text: This is a practical command map, not a full generated API reference.

docs.title: Core Commands
docs.text:
kabot config
kabot gateway
kabot agent
kabot doctor

docs.title: Config And Setup
docs.text:
kabot config
kabot config --token-mode boros
kabot config --token-mode hemat
kabot config --token-saver
kabot config --no-token-saver

docs.title: Gateway
docs.text:
kabot gateway
kabot gateway --port 18790

docs.title: Agent
docs.text:
kabot agent -m "Hello Kabot"
kabot agent

docs.title: Doctor
docs.text:
kabot doctor --fix
kabot doctor routing
kabot doctor smoke-agent --smoke-timeout 30

docs.title: Auth
docs.text:
kabot auth status
kabot auth login openai
kabot auth login google --method oauth

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
kabot agents list
kabot agents add work --name "Work Agent" --model openai/gpt-4o
kabot agents delete work

docs.title: Docs Build
docs.text:
pip install ".[docs]"
mkdocs serve
mkdocs build --strict

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: This page summarizes the most important configuration domains.

docs.title: Runtime Themes
docs.text: Kabot configuration usually clusters into these groups: - models and auth - gateway and exposure - channels and bindings - skills and env values - memory and persistence - agent routing - security and trust

docs.title: Model/Auth
docs.text: Defines: - provider login state - primary model - fallback chain - provider-specific auth method

docs.title: Gateway
docs.text: Defines: - port - bind behavior - auth token and scopes - optional Tailscale-related runtime mode

docs.title: Skills
docs.text: Defines: - entries - enabled/disabled state - env values - install/setup choices

docs.title: Channels
docs.text: Defines: - credentials - allowFrom - channel instance settings - channel-to-agent relationships

docs.title: Agents
docs.text: Defines: - agent IDs and names - workspace path - model assignment - default agent - channel bindings

docs.title: Memory
docs.text: Defines: - memory mode and footprint - retrieval strategy expectations - persistence behavior

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: Learn the wizard first, then map the wizard menus back to the underlying config concepts.

docs.title: Dashboard Routes Reference
docs.text: This page summarizes the main dashboard-facing routes and their purpose.

docs.title: Main Surface
docs.text:
• /dashboard

This is the browser-facing shell for the operator UI.

docs.title: Common JSON / operator endpoints
docs.text:
Examples include surfaces for: - status - chat history - SSE chat stream - sessions - nodes - config - control

Representative routes include:

/dashboard/api/status
/dashboard/api/chat/history
/dashboard/api/chat/stream
/dashboard/api/sessions
/dashboard/api/nodes
/dashboard/api/config
/dashboard/api/control

docs.title: Common Mutation Endpoints
docs.text:
Examples of dashboard-driven mutation flows include: - sessions clear/delete - node start/stop/restart - chat send - control actions - cron and skill operator actions through partial-driven routes

These should be treated as operator write surfaces, not casual public endpoints.

docs.title: Partial Routes
docs.text:
Kabot uses partial routes for panel updates in the dashboard, including families such as: - metrics - alerts - health - cost - charts - channels - cron - models - skills - sub-agent activity - git log style operator panels

These partials are important because they allow: - in-place refresh - lower UI cost - better dashboard responsiveness than full-page reload loops

docs.title: Scope Expectations
docs.text: Typically: - operator.read is enough for visibility routes - operator.write is required for mutation routes - ingress.write is for webhook ingestion, not normal dashboard browsing

docs.title: Recommended Security Model
docs.text: For a real deployment:

docs.title: Read-only operator
docs.text:
Use: - operator.read

Good for: - dashboards - observers - on-call status viewers

docs.title: Active operator
docs.text:
Use: - operator.read - operator.write

Good for: - admins - maintainers - people who need to trigger actions

docs.title: Ingress-only integration
docs.text:
Use: - ingress.write

Good for: - webhook producers - adapter/bridge integrations

docs.title: Query Token Note
docs.text:
Dashboard token-in-query usage is intentionally limited to dashboard-facing routes.

Do not assume that same convenience path should be used for generic ingress/webhook surfaces.

docs.title: Panel Families You Should Know
docs.text: Family Typical Purpose metrics top bar CPU/RAM/disk/runtime health alerts operator warning banner health system/gateway state summary cost cost and token usage windows charts historical visual breakdowns channels configured channel surface cron job visibility and actions models available/runtime model visibility skills skill state and actions

docs.title: Operational Advice
docs.text:
• never expose dashboard write routes casually
  • separate read viewers from write operators where possible
  • prefer scoped tokens over broad legacy full-access tokens
  • combine dashboard protection with network protection, not just token protection

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: Treat dashboard URLs as an operator surface and protect them accordingly.
